title: Information Hierarchy in Many-Body Berry Phase
abstract: Many-body topology is a central concept in modern theories of solids, and identifying effective degrees of freedom that capture it is important both fundamentally and practically. This work studies the extent to which geometric information of an interacting many-body ground state can be inferred from a finite number of local correlations. Starting from the Resta formula, $ z=\left\langle \exp\!\left(\frac{2πi}{L}\hat X\right)\right\rangle$, we view $\log z$ as the cumulant generating function and establish a generic information hierarchy across cumulant orders. We show that, for an $N$-particle system, even complete knowledge of all density correlators up to order $N-1$ does not, in general, uniquely determine the Berry phase $γ=\operatorname{Im}\log z \, (\mathrm{mod}\ 2π)$. In the thermodynamic limit, the statement becomes stronger: no finite set of local correlators suffices to determine the global holonomy. We also identify two exceptional yes-go cases in which the hierarchy is broken. First, for quasi-free models, all cumulants are determined by the particle two-point correlation function. Second, symmetry-enforced constraints can reduce the infinite cumulant sum entering $\log z$ to finite information. The argument is analytic and does not rely on a specific microscopic Hamiltonian. Our results clarify a limitation of approaches based on local degrees of freedom for many-body holonomy and provide a minimal framework for distinguishing when global holonomies are encoded in local correlations and when they are not. We also comment on the possibility of analogous hierarchies in other contexts, such as the quantum marginal problem in quantum information theory and many-body scattering problems. Finally, we discuss implications for future numerical work, including machine-learning approaches to the search for topological phases.
summary: This theoretical work establishes a rigorous information hierarchy showing that global topological invariants, like the Berry phase, generally cannot be determined solely from a finite number of local correlation measurements in interacting many-body systems. The authors identify that this limitation is overcome only in special cases, such as when the system is quasi-free or when strong symmetries are present.
main_problem: The central problem is determining the extent to which the geometric information of an interacting many-body ground state, specifically the Berry phase, can be inferred from a finite set of local correlation functions.
main_result: In general, no finite set of local correlators is sufficient to uniquely determine the global Berry phase in the thermodynamic limit, establishing a strict information hierarchy. Exceptions exist for quasi-free models or when symmetries impose strong constraints.
method: The analysis uses the Resta formula, viewing the logarithm of the partition function as a cumulant generating function, and systematically compares the constraints imposed by low-order versus high-order density correlators.
model_or_system: The study applies to general interacting many-body quantum systems, using the Berry phase derived from the expectation value of a unitary operator (LSM) threading a flux. Examples include analyzing chain rings and considering general fermionic Hamiltonians.
key_observables: The Berry phase ($\gamma$), the cumulants ($\kappa_p$), and various $p$-point density correlators ($\langle \hat{n}_{j_1} \cdots \hat{n}_{j_p} angle$).
important_parameters: The system size ($L$), the number of particles ($N$), and the order of the correlation ($p$). The thermodynamic limit ($L 	o \infty$) is crucial.
main_assumptions: The argument is analytic and does not rely on a specific microscopic Hamiltonian, though specific cases like quasi-free models or symmetry constraints are analyzed.
figures_summary: The notes mention Table I, which explicitly calculates exact cumulants ($\kappa_p$) for various $(L, N)$ pairs, demonstrating the growth of these cumulants with system size.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the general information hierarchy using the cumulant generating function approach, presents a concrete counterexample (e.g., $L=6$ chain ring) showing the failure of low-order correlators to determine the phase, and then details the two exceptional 'yes-go' cases (quasi-free and symmetry-enforced) where the hierarchy closes.
why_interesting: This work provides a fundamental theoretical boundary condition for how local measurements constrain global topological properties, which is critical for designing experimental protocols or interpreting numerical simulations of topological phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
